title: Abdominal Ultrasound
steps: Ask patient to fast 4–6 hours prior to reduce bowel gas
Position patient supine on examination table
Apply ultrasound gel generously on abdomen
Place transducer on abdomen; start at right upper quadrant
Scan liver in longitudinal and transverse planes systematically
Move to gallbladder, bile ducts, pancreas, spleen, kidneys, aorta, bladder
Apply gentle pressure to displace bowel gas if needed
Document findings with labeled images and measurements
Wipe gel off patient and discuss findings

title: Suturing a Wound
steps: Clean wound thoroughly with normal saline; assess depth and edges
Inject local anesthetic (Lidocaine 1%) around wound edges
Set up sterile drape field; wear sterile gloves
Choose suture: Nylon/Prolene for skin, Vicryl for deeper tissue layers
Hold needle with needle driver at 90° angle to skin
Enter skin 3–5mm from wound edge; exit symmetrically on opposite side
Tie square knot with 3 throws minimum; cut tails at 3–4mm
Place sutures 5–10mm apart along entire wound length
Apply antibiotic ointment and sterile dressing
Advise removal timing: face 5 days, limbs 7–10 days, back 10–14 days

title: 12-Lead ECG
steps: Position patient supine, relaxed, with limbs straight and uncrossed
Clean and dry skin; shave hair if electrode contact is poor
Place limb leads: RA (right arm), LA (left arm), RL (right leg), LL (left leg)
Place V1 at 4th intercostal space, right sternal border
Place V2 at 4th intercostal space, left sternal border
Place V3 between V2 and V4
Place V4 at 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line
Place V5 at anterior axillary line, same level as V4
Place V6 at midaxillary line, same level as V4–V5
Ask patient to lie still and not talk during recording
Run ECG and confirm clean trace with no artifact
Label strip with patient name, date, time, and clinical indication

title: Lumbar Puncture (Spinal Tap)
steps: Check contraindications: raised ICP, bleeding disorder, skin infection at site
Position patient in lateral decubitus with knees tucked to chest OR seated leaning forward
Identify L3–L4 or L4–L5 interspace at the level of iliac crests
Clean skin with chlorhexidine in wide circular motion; apply sterile drape
Inject local anesthetic into skin and deeper tissue layer
Insert spinal needle bevel-up at slight angle toward umbilicus
Advance slowly until a 'give' sensation indicates dura puncture
Remove stylet and confirm CSF drip
Measure opening pressure with manometer
Collect 3–4 tubes of 1–2ml each; label sequentially
Reinsert stylet before withdrawing needle
Apply dressing; keep patient flat for 1 hour post-procedure

title: Chest Auscultation
steps: Warm stethoscope diaphragm before placing on patient skin
Ask patient to breathe slowly and deeply through the mouth
Auscultate systematically: top to bottom, left to right, comparing both sides
Cover 4 anterior positions each side and 5 posterior positions each side
Note breath sounds: vesicular (normal), bronchial, absent, or reduced
Note added sounds: wheeze (airways narrowing), crackles (fluid or fibrosis), pleural rub
Document findings per lung zone clearly

title: Abdominal Palpation
steps: Ensure patient is relaxed and supine with arms by their sides
Warm hands and kneel or sit at patient level
Begin with light palpation across all 9 abdominal regions; watch face for pain
Proceed to deep palpation to assess organs
Palpate liver: right hand below right costal margin; ask patient to inhale
Palpate spleen: right hand at left costal margin during inspiration
Palpate kidneys: bimanual technique — one hand behind, one in front (balloting)
Check for tenderness, guarding, rigidity, and rebound tenderness
Percuss for shifting dullness if ascites is suspected

title: Normal Vaginal Delivery
steps: Confirm full cervical dilation (10cm); presenting part visible
Support perineum with one hand; apply gentle counter-pressure on fetal head
Guide head to extend slowly under pubic symphysis
Once head delivers, check for nuchal cord — loop over head if present
Allow head to restitute naturally to face one thigh
Guide head downward to deliver anterior shoulder, then upward for posterior shoulder
Support baby as body delivers; note exact delivery time
Double clamp cord and cut between clamps
Deliver placenta with controlled cord traction plus maternal pushing
Inspect placenta for completeness; assess for perineal tears
Suture tears if present; monitor maternal bleeding closely for 1 hour

title: Plaster Cast Application
steps: Confirm fracture position on X-ray; ensure reduction is adequate
Apply stockinette over the limb extending beyond both cast edges
Wrap cotton wool padding evenly with extra over all bony prominences
Dip plaster of Paris bandage in warm water; squeeze out excess water
Apply in smooth overlapping layers with 50% overlap, molding gently
Fold stockinette back over edges and seal with a final plaster layer
Hold limb in correct anatomical position until cast sets (5–10 minutes)
Check 5 P's after casting: Pain, Pallor, Pulse, Paralysis, Paresthesia
Advise patient to elevate limb and return immediately if fingers or toes swell or go numb

title: IV Cannulation (Peripheral)
steps: Gather equipment: appropriate gauge cannula, tourniquet, flush syringe, tape, gloves
Apply tourniquet 10–15cm above the intended insertion site
Select vein; clean site with chlorhexidine or alcohol swab and allow to dry
Insert cannula bevel-up at 15–30° angle to skin
Confirm blood flashback in the cannula chamber
Advance plastic cannula forward while withdrawing the metal stylet
Release tourniquet; connect IV line or flush with 5ml normal saline
Secure with transparent dressing; document date, time, and gauge size

title: Intramuscular (IM) Injection
steps: Verify medication, dose, route, expiry, and patient identity (5 Rights)
Select injection site: deltoid, vastus lateralis, or ventrogluteal
Wash hands and apply non-sterile gloves
Clean site with alcohol swab in circular motion; allow to dry fully
Insert needle swiftly at 90° with firm motion
Aspirate by pulling back plunger — if blood appears, withdraw and restart
Inject medication slowly and steadily
Withdraw needle smoothly; apply gentle pressure without rubbing
Dispose of needle immediately in sharps container

title: Subcutaneous (SC) Injection
steps: Confirm medication and patient identity (insulin, heparin, vaccines)
Select site: abdomen, upper arm, or thigh; rotate sites each time
Clean with alcohol swab; allow to dry completely
Pinch skin firmly to lift subcutaneous tissue away from muscle
Insert needle at 45–90° depending on patient body composition
Inject medication slowly and steadily
Release pinched skin before withdrawing needle
Apply light pressure; dispose needle in sharps container immediately

title: Nasogastric (NG) Tube Insertion
steps: Explain procedure to patient and position upright or semi-reclined
Measure tube length: tip of nose to earlobe to xiphoid process; mark on tube
Lubricate tube tip generously with water-soluble gel
Insert through chosen nostril and advance toward back of throat
Ask patient to swallow repeatedly as tube advances past the pharynx
Advance to marked length
Confirm placement by aspirating gastric contents and checking pH (<5.5) or via CXR
Secure tube to nose with tape; document length at nostril, date, and time

title: Urinary Catheterization
steps: Maintain strict aseptic technique throughout entire procedure
Position patient and drape appropriately for privacy and access
Clean urethral meatus with antiseptic solution (front to back in females)
Lubricate catheter tip generously with sterile gel
Insert catheter gently until urine flows freely; advance 2cm further
Inflate balloon with specified volume of sterile water per catheter label
Connect to closed drainage bag and secure tubing to inner thigh
Document catheter size, balloon volume, initial urine output, and time

title: Oxygen Administration
steps: Assess SpO2 via pulse oximeter and measure respiratory rate
Select delivery device based on required FiO2:
– Nasal cannula: 1–6 L/min (24–44% FiO2) for mild hypoxia
– Simple face mask: 6–10 L/min for moderate hypoxia
– Non-rebreather mask: 10–15 L/min for severe hypoxia
Set correct flow rate on oxygen cylinder or wall supply
Apply device and confirm proper fit and seal
Recheck SpO2 after 5 minutes; target >94% (88–92% for COPD patients)
Document flow rate, delivery device used, and SpO2 response

title: Basic Life Support (BLS / CPR)
steps: Confirm scene safety before approaching patient
Check responsiveness: tap shoulders and shout loudly
Call for emergency help or send bystander to call
Check for breathing for no more than 10 seconds
Begin chest compressions: heel of hand on center of chest
Compress at least 5cm deep at a rate of 100–120 per minute
Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions (30:2 ratio)
Attach AED as soon as available and follow all voice prompts
Continue CPR until patient shows signs of life, AED advises no shock, or help arrives

title: Blood Glucose Measurement
steps: Wash patient hands with soap and warm water; dry completely
Insert a fresh test strip into the glucometer
Prick the lateral side of fingertip with a lancet
Wipe away the first drop of blood with clean gauze
Apply the second blood drop to the test strip
Read the result within the device's stated time window
Record value with timestamp; normal fasting range: 4.0–5.5 mmol/L (72–99 mg/dL)
Dispose of lancet in sharps container

title: Blood Pressure Measurement
steps: Have patient sit quietly for 5 minutes with arm supported at heart level
Apply cuff 2–3cm above the antecubital fossa (inner elbow crease)
Inflate cuff to approximately 30 mmHg above expected systolic pressure
Deflate slowly at 2–3 mmHg per second while listening with stethoscope
Record first Korotkoff sound as systolic pressure
Record disappearance of sound as diastolic pressure
Take 2 readings 1–2 minutes apart and average the values
Normal reading: below 120/80 mmHg; flag if above 140/90 mmHg

title: Wound Dressing Change
steps: Assemble sterile field: gloves, normal saline, gauze, appropriate dressing
Remove old dressing; moisten with saline if adhered to wound bed
Observe wound: note size, depth, color, odor, exudate, surrounding skin condition
Irrigate wound with normal saline using a 20ml syringe
Select appropriate dressing: dry gauze, foam, hydrocolloid, or silver dressing based on wound type
Apply dressing without excessive tension; do not over-tape
Document wound findings and schedule next dressing change

title: Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Sampling
steps: Perform Allen's test to confirm collateral circulation before radial artery puncture
Position patient's wrist extended with a roll of gauze underneath
Clean radial artery site with alcohol swab; allow to dry
Insert heparinized syringe needle bevel-up at 45–60° directly over artery pulsation
Allow blood to fill syringe passively — do not pull back plunger
Withdraw needle when 1–2ml collected; apply firm pressure for 5 minutes
Expel air bubbles from syringe immediately; cap and mix gently
Label and transport on ice to analyzer within 15 minutes
Document FiO2 and patient temperature at time of sampling

title: Ophthalmoscopy (Fundus Examination)
steps: Dim room lights; dilate pupil with tropicamide drops if needed
Ask patient to fix gaze on a distant target
Set ophthalmoscope to 0 diopters; use right eye to examine patient's right eye
Approach from 15° temporal to patient's line of sight
Locate optic disc first: round, pale orange, clear margins
Follow each vessel outward from disc in 4 quadrants
Examine macula last: ask patient to look directly at light
Note: disc color, cup-to-disc ratio, vessel changes, hemorrhages, exudates
Repeat for left eye using your left eye

title: Ear Examination (Otoscopy)
steps: Select appropriate speculum size for patient age
For adults: pull pinna upward and backward to straighten the canal
For children under 3: pull pinna downward and backward
Gently insert speculum into the outer third of the ear canal only
Inspect canal for wax, foreign bodies, discharge, or inflammation
Visualize tympanic membrane: note color (pearly grey = normal), light reflex, landmarks
Check for perforation, retraction, bulging, or fluid behind eardrum
Document findings for both ears

title: Throat Examination
steps: Ask patient to open mouth wide and say 'Ahh' to lower tongue
Use tongue depressor to depress mid-tongue; illuminate with pen torch
Inspect tonsils: size (graded 1–4), color, surface, presence of exudate or crypts
Inspect posterior pharyngeal wall: redness, discharge, lymphoid follicles
Check uvula position: midline or deviated
Inspect soft palate movement: should elevate symmetrically on phonation
Palpate neck for lymphadenopathy: submandibular, anterior and posterior cervical chains
Document all findings clearly

title: Peak Flow Measurement
steps: Attach clean mouthpiece to peak flow meter; set marker to zero
Ask patient to stand or sit upright
Instruct patient to inhale as deeply as possible
Seal lips tightly around mouthpiece and blow out as hard and fast as possible
Record the reading on the gauge
Repeat 3 times; record the best of 3 readings
Compare with patient's personal best or predicted value for age, sex, height
Normal: >80% predicted; moderate: 50–80%; severe: <50%

title: Intravenous Fluid Administration
steps: Confirm fluid type, volume, and rate as prescribed
Check IV fluid bag: clarity, expiry, leaks, and correct label
Spike the bag with IV giving set; prime the line fully to remove all air
Connect to patient's existing IV cannula after confirming patency
Set drip rate manually or program infusion pump
Verify flow: 1 litre over 8 hours = 42 drops/minute (standard giving set)
Monitor site every 30 minutes for swelling, redness, or pain (extravasation)
Document fluid type, volume, start time, and rate

title: Pleural Aspiration (Thoracocentesis)
steps: Confirm effusion on CXR or ultrasound; mark insertion site
Position patient sitting upright and leaning forward over a table
Identify site: one intercostal space below fluid level, mid-scapular line
Clean with chlorhexidine; apply sterile drape
Inject local anesthetic down to pleura; insert needle just above rib (avoid neurovascular bundle below)
Advance needle while aspirating until fluid is obtained
Connect to 3-way stopcock and syringe; drain fluid slowly
Do not remove more than 1.5 litres at once (risk of re-expansion pulmonary edema)
Withdraw needle; apply dressing; obtain CXR post-procedure

title: Chest Tube Insertion
steps: Confirm indication: pneumothorax, hemothorax, or large effusion
Position patient supine with arm raised behind head; identify 4th–5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line
Clean and drape; infiltrate with local anesthetic down to pleura
Make 2–3cm horizontal incision over top of rib
Blunt dissect with clamp through chest wall; puncture pleura with controlled push
Insert finger to confirm pleural space and clear of lung
Insert chest tube (clamped) guided by finger; direct posteriorly and superiorly
Unclamp and connect to underwater seal drain
Confirm position: tube fogs, water swings with breathing
Secure tube with suture and occlusive dressing; obtain CXR

title: Neonatal Resuscitation
steps: Prepare warm, dry environment and resuscitation equipment before delivery
At birth: dry baby, assess tone, breathing, and color simultaneously
Stimulate by rubbing back if not breathing; reposition airway
If not breathing within 60 seconds: start positive pressure ventilation (PPV) at 40–60 breaths/min
Check chest rise with each PPV breath; correct mask seal if absent
If HR <60 after 30 seconds of adequate PPV: start chest compressions 3:1 ratio with ventilations
Administer IV adrenaline (epinephrine) if HR remains <60 after 30 seconds of compressions
Reassess HR every 30 seconds; continue until spontaneous breathing established
Document times of interventions, APGAR scores, and response

title: Antenatal (Prenatal) Examination
steps: Confirm gestational age by LMP and/or ultrasound
Measure weight and blood pressure at every visit
Measure symphysis-fundal height (SFH) from 24 weeks: should equal weeks of gestation ±2cm
Palpate uterus using Leopold's Maneuvers: assess lie, presentation, position, engagement
Auscultate fetal heart rate: normal 110–160 bpm
Check for peripheral edema, varicosities, and signs of anemia (conjunctival pallor)
Perform urine dipstick: check for protein and glucose
Review blood results: Hb, blood group, syphilis, HIV, hepatitis B at booking
Counsel on danger signs: bleeding, reduced fetal movement, headache, visual disturbance

title: Intravenous Drug Administration
steps: Verify 5 Rights: right patient, drug, dose, route, and time
Check for drug allergies; review compatibility with existing IV fluids
Prepare drug aseptically: reconstitute powder with correct diluent if needed
Draw up into syringe; label clearly
Flush IV cannula with 5ml normal saline to confirm patency
Administer IV bolus slowly over stated time (e.g., morphine over 5 minutes)
Flush again with 5ml saline after administration
Monitor patient for 5–10 minutes for adverse reactions
Document drug, dose, time, route, batch number, and patient response

title: Skin Biopsy (Punch Biopsy)
steps: Identify lesion; document site with photograph if available
Clean site with alcohol swab; allow to dry
Inject local anesthetic (Lidocaine 1%) subcutaneously around lesion
Stretch skin perpendicular to skin tension lines
Press punch biopsy tool firmly and rotate in one direction only until resistance reduces
Lift core with needle or fine forceps from the base without crushing
Cut base with fine scissors
Place specimen in formalin-filled container immediately; label correctly
Close defect with 1–2 sutures or allow to heal by secondary intention
Apply dressing; send specimen with completed histology request form

title: Ascitic Tap (Paracentesis)
steps: Confirm ascites on clinical examination and ultrasound
Position patient supine; identify insertion site in left iliac fossa (avoids inferior epigastric vessels)
Clean with chlorhexidine; apply sterile drape
Inject local anesthetic down to peritoneum
Insert cannula or needle using Z-track technique to prevent leak
Connect to drainage bag; allow to drain slowly
For diagnostic tap: collect 20–50ml for cytology, protein, albumin, culture
For therapeutic tap: drain up to 5 litres; give albumin replacement (8g per litre drained)
Withdraw needle; apply firm pressure and dressing
Monitor vitals post-procedure; watch for hypotension

title: Knee Joint Aspiration
steps: Confirm effusion by patellar tap or ballottement
Position patient supine with knee slightly flexed on pillow
Clean with chlorhexidine; apply sterile drape and gloves
Inject local anesthetic into skin lateral to patella
Insert needle at mid-patella laterally, aiming toward joint space at 45°
Aspirate fluid: note color (clear = normal, yellow = infection, bloody = trauma or hemarthrosis)
Drain as much fluid as possible with syringe
If injecting corticosteroid: change syringe without moving needle
Withdraw needle; apply pressure and dressing
Send fluid for microscopy, culture, and crystal analysis

title: Cervical Smear (Pap Smear)
steps: Confirm indication; ensure patient has not menstruated or used vaginal products for 24 hours
Position patient in lithotomy; ensure privacy and comfort
Insert appropriately sized speculum gently; visualize the cervix
Use Cervex-Brush: insert central bristles into endocervical canal
Rotate brush clockwise 5 full turns to collect cells
Rinse brush head into liquid-based cytology vial by rotating and pressing against vial wall
Withdraw speculum; assist patient to sit up
Label vial with patient name, DOB, LMP, and send with completed request form

title: Endotracheal Intubation
steps: Pre-oxygenate patient with 100% O2 for 3 minutes via bag-valve mask
Prepare equipment: ETT (correct size), laryngoscope, syringe, tape, suction
Position patient: sniffing position (head extended, neck flexed) on flat surface
Administer induction agent and neuromuscular blocker (RSI if needed)
Hold laryngoscope in left hand; insert blade into right side of mouth and sweep tongue left
Lift laryngoscope up and forward (not rotating back) to expose glottis
Visualize vocal cords; insert ETT through cords to correct depth (21cm women, 23cm men)
Inflate cuff with 5–10ml air; confirm placement by EtCO2 waveform and bilateral breath sounds
Secure tube with tape or holder; document tube size and depth at teeth
Obtain CXR to confirm position (tip should be 2–4cm above carina)

title: Newborn Examination
steps: Perform in warm room within 24 hours of birth
Assess APGAR score at 1 and 5 minutes: tone, pulse, grimace, appearance, respiration
Measure weight, length, and head circumference; plot on growth chart
Inspect head: fontanelles, sutures, caput, cephalohematoma
Examine eyes: red reflex bilaterally (absence suggests cataract or retinoblastoma)
Inspect palate for cleft with finger and torch
Auscultate heart: murmurs, rate, rhythm
Auscultate lungs bilaterally
Palpate abdomen: liver, spleen, kidneys; check umbilical cord
Examine hips: Barlow and Ortolani maneuvers for developmental dysplasia
Inspect spine: sacral dimple, neural tube defects
Check genitalia, anus, and limbs for anomalies
Elicit Moro, grasp, and rooting reflexes

title: Neurological Examination
steps: Assess consciousness level using GCS: Eyes (1–4), Verbal (1–5), Motor (1–6)
Test orientation: person, place, time
Examine cranial nerves I–XII systematically
Assess motor system: bulk, tone, power (MRC scale 0–5) in all 4 limbs
Test reflexes: biceps (C5/C6), triceps (C7), knee (L3/L4), ankle (S1), plantar response
Assess coordination: finger-nose test, heel-shin test, dysdiadochokinesia
Test sensation: light touch, pinprick, vibration, proprioception
Assess gait: observe walking, heel-to-toe walking, Romberg's test
Document findings by region and side clearly

title: Chest X-Ray Interpretation
steps: Check patient name, date, and view (PA vs AP); note any rotation or poor inspiration
Assess bones: ribs, clavicles, scapulae, vertebrae for fractures or lesions
Examine trachea: should be midline
Assess heart: size (cardiothoracic ratio <0.5 on PA view), borders, shape
Examine hila: position, size, density
Review lung fields systematically: upper, mid, lower zones bilaterally
Look for: consolidation, effusion, pneumothorax, masses, lymphadenopathy
Examine diaphragm: hemidiaphragm levels, costophrenic angles (blunting = effusion)
Check soft tissues and visible abdomen
Formulate findings into a structured report

title: Spinal Immobilization
steps: Assume spinal injury in any unconscious trauma patient or significant mechanism of injury
Manually stabilize head and neck in neutral alignment immediately
Apply correctly sized rigid cervical collar
Log-roll patient as a unit with 3–4 people: one controls head, others control body
During log-roll: inspect back for wounds, deformity, or step deformities
Place patient on spinal board or scoop stretcher
Secure patient with straps at chest, pelvis, and thighs
Use head blocks and tape across forehead and chin to restrict head movement
Reassess neurological function: motor and sensation in all 4 limbs

title: Ear Syringing (Ear Irrigation)
steps: Confirm no perforation, discharge, or previous ear surgery before proceeding
Instruct patient to use olive oil or sodium bicarbonate drops for 3–5 days prior to soften wax
Warm water to body temperature (37°C) to avoid caloric stimulation and vertigo
Seat patient; place kidney bowl under ear and against neck
Pull pinna upward and backward to straighten canal
Direct stream of water toward posterior-superior wall of canal (not directly at drum)
Use gentle continuous pressure; check canal between syringes
Once clear, dry canal with soft tissue or suction
Perform otoscopy to confirm drum is intact and canal is clear
Advise to avoid water in ear for 24 hours

title: Anaphylaxis Management
steps: Remove or stop the triggering agent immediately
Call for emergency help; note time of onset
Administer Adrenaline (Epinephrine) 0.5mg IM into outer thigh (anterolateral) immediately
Position patient: supine with legs raised unless breathing difficulty
Give high-flow oxygen 15L/min via non-rebreather mask
Establish IV access; give 500–1000ml normal saline bolus for hypotension
Repeat adrenaline every 5 minutes if no improvement
Give IV Chlorphenamine (antihistamine) and IV Hydrocortisone as secondary measures
Monitor continuously: BP, HR, SpO2, respiratory rate
Observe for minimum 6–12 hours for biphasic reaction; admit if severe

title: Cardioversion (DC Cardioversion)
steps: Confirm indication: AF, SVT, VT with pulse (not for VF — use defibrillation)
Obtain informed consent; ensure anticoagulation if AF >48 hours
Ensure IV access; attach ECG monitoring and pulse oximeter
Sedate patient: Midazolam or Propofol with airway support ready
Apply self-adhesive pads: one below right clavicle, one apex (V5 position)
Select synchronized mode (SYNC) — critical to avoid R-on-T phenomenon
Select energy: AF 120–200J biphasic; SVT/VT 50–100J
Ensure all personnel stand clear of patient and bed
Deliver shock; immediately assess rhythm and pulse
Repeat with escalating energy if unsuccessful; document rhythm after each shock

title: Male Genital Examination
steps: Ensure privacy, chaperone present, and patient consent obtained
Inspect penis: skin, meatus position (hypospadias), foreskin retraction, discharge
Inspect scrotum: skin, symmetry (left side normally lower)
Palpate each testis: size, shape, consistency, tenderness
Palpate epididymis: posterior to testis; check for tenderness or cysts
Transilluminate any scrotal swelling: hydrocele transilluminates, solid mass does not
Check for varicocele: palpate while patient stands and performs Valsalva
Palpate inguinal canal for hernias
Document all findings systematically

title: Thyroid Examination
steps: Inspect neck from front: look for visible goitre; ask patient to swallow (thyroid moves up)
Offer glass of water; watch for thyroid movement on swallowing
Palpate from behind with both hands: assess size, shape, symmetry, surface texture
Check for nodules: single or multiple, hard or soft, tender or non-tender
Percuss over sternum for retrosternal extension
Auscultate over thyroid for bruit (suggests hypervascular Graves' disease)
Assess for signs of hyper/hypothyroidism: tremor, tachycardia, eye signs, slow relaxing reflexes
Palpate cervical lymph nodes for lymphadenopathy

title: Urinalysis (Urine Dipstick)
steps: Request midstream urine (MSU) sample; ensure clean catch technique
Check urine visually: color, clarity, and any obvious abnormalities
Remove dipstick from sealed container; do not touch reagent pads
Dip stick fully into urine for 1–2 seconds; remove and hold horizontally
Wait specified time per manufacturer (usually 60 seconds for most pads)
Compare pads to color chart in good lighting; read in correct order
Record results: pH, protein, glucose, ketones, blood, nitrites, leukocytes, bilirubin, urobilinogen
Positive nitrites + leukocytes = UTI until proven otherwise; send for culture
Document results, collection method, and any follow-up action

title: Intraosseous (IO) Access
steps: Indicated when IV access cannot be obtained rapidly in emergency (especially in children)
Select site: proximal tibia (2cm below tibial tuberosity, medial flat surface) or proximal humerus
Clean site with chlorhexidine; apply local anesthetic if patient is conscious
Insert IO needle or drill at 90° to bone surface with firm controlled pressure
Confirm placement: needle stands upright without support; aspiration of bone marrow; fluids flow without extravasation
Flush with 10ml normal saline; expect slight resistance then free flow
Secure needle and connect to IV fluid or syringe driver
All IV drugs and fluids can be administered via IO route
Remove as soon as IV access is established (within 24 hours maximum)

title: Intercostal Nerve Block
steps: Identify indication: rib fracture pain, post-thoracotomy, chest drain insertion
Position patient: sitting or lateral decubitus with arm raised
Identify target ribs; mark insertion points at posterior angle of ribs
Clean with chlorhexidine and drape
Insert needle at lower border of rib, walking needle below rib edge to inferior margin
Advance 2–3mm past rib; aspirate to exclude vessel or lung puncture
Inject 3–5ml of Bupivacaine 0.5% per level
Block one level above and below target rib due to overlap
Monitor for pneumothorax (rare complication) with SpO2 and CXR if concerned

title: Intrapartum Fetal Monitoring (CTG Interpretation)
steps: Apply external CTG monitor: Doppler probe over fetal back, tocometer on fundus
Record for minimum 20 minutes; ensure adequate trace quality
Assess baseline fetal heart rate: normal 110–160 bpm
Assess baseline variability: normal 5–25 bpm variation
Identify accelerations: rise >15 bpm for >15 seconds (reassuring sign)
Identify decelerations: early (with contractions, normal), late (after contraction peak, concerning), variable (cord compression)
Classify CTG as: Normal (all 4 features reassuring), Suspicious (1 non-reassuring), Pathological (2+ non-reassuring or 1 abnormal)
For pathological CTG: fetal blood sampling or expedite delivery
Document interpretation and clinical decision with timestamp

title: Ophthalmology: Visual Acuity Testing
steps: Use Snellen chart at 6 metres (or equivalent LogMAR chart)
Test each eye separately; cover the other eye fully without pressing on it
Patient wears their usual glasses or contact lenses if distance-corrected
Ask patient to read smallest line they can see clearly from top to bottom
Record as fraction: 6/6 (normal), 6/12 (sees at 6m what normal person sees at 12m)
If unable to read top line at 6m: move patient to 3m and retest
If still unable: test counting fingers (CF), hand movements (HM), perception of light (PL)
Repeat with pinhole occluder: improvement suggests refractive error; no improvement suggests pathology
Document results for each eye separately

title: Fracture Reduction (Closed Manipulation)
steps: Review X-ray: confirm fracture type, displacement, and angulation
Obtain informed consent; explain procedure and risks
Administer adequate analgesia: IV morphine or haematoma block with Lidocaine
Apply steady longitudinal traction along the axis of the limb
Recreate the deformity slightly to disimpact the fracture fragments
Reverse the deformity with controlled manipulation to achieve reduction
Maintain reduction while assistant applies backslab or cast
Confirm reduction with post-manipulation X-ray in 2 views
Check neurovascular status distally post-reduction: pulses, sensation, capillary refill
Arrange follow-up X-ray in 1 week to check for re-displacement

title: Neonatal Jaundice Assessment
steps: Assess onset timing: jaundice within 24 hours is always pathological
Examine in natural daylight; press skin gently and observe underlying color
Estimate extent using Kramer's zones: face (zone 1) to feet (zone 5) as levels progress
Measure serum bilirubin (SBR) — transcutaneous bilirubinometer is screening only; confirm with blood if above threshold
Plot SBR on age-in-hours treatment threshold chart (NICE or local guidelines)
Check blood group, Coombs test, FBC, and reticulocyte count for haemolytic causes
Start phototherapy if SBR is at or above treatment line
Recheck SBR 4–6 hours after starting phototherapy
Exchange transfusion if SBR reaches exchange line despite phototherapy
Ensure adequate feeding (jaundice worsens with dehydration)
